item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Human, come hither, and experience the most incredulous of excretory experiences!

+1

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image source is describe here and is public domain, therefore CC Compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love SCPs that are both compelling and make me laugh out loud. This fits both categories. Amazing SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Flameice2, if you want a response from a particular article's author, the best way to contact them is via Wikidot PM. Authors don't always check their page discussions and wikidot doesn't have an automatic notification system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I'm making a SCP, and i was wondering if i could reference yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if you take an item out of the house? Does the effect end, or do you just have a pissed-off god of tampons or whatever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very belatedly thirding. There's no real substance or hook here. It reads like a (admittedly well-executed) prompt from chat. "Hey guys, what if there was this house, see? And all of the objects in it thought that they were gods of whatever they were. And, wait for it… they argued with each other!"

For whatever reason, this rubs me the wrong way, so I'mma downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interview devolves into an argument between SCP-2257-2 and its articles of clothing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belatedly seconding this. I was gonna write more, but this gets right to the heart of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is silly. This is far too silly!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly what I was thinking. Snow removal is also needed in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couldn't upvote enough.

So much +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though I think that series 3 has too many silly SCPs, this one really made my day
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, no! It's fine; the name works. I hadn't actually noticed that I had left it blank, so, sorry if I've caused trouble. Thank you though, for fixing it, even if you hadn't had the correct permissions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although it might be a bit late for it, noting the title change. Sorry, InsipidParoxysm. I hadn't seen you since you posted it so I just named the article after the draft name.

item.title: Re: Listing
item.content:encoded: Bryx wasn't given staff permission to do that, so if you want a different name in the slot, go ahead and change it. It's your article, after all.

item.title: Re: Listing
item.content:encoded:
Someone who wasn't the author named it after the draft it went by in the editing phase.

For the record, it's the author that should've done the naming, and I've contacted the person involved.

item.title: Re: Listing
item.content:encoded: Huh, that's weird. When I was looking through the listings last night it definitely was still showing [ACCESS DENIED] But now it's showing as House God. Strange :P

item.title: Re: Listing
item.content:encoded:
Shoot, I swear I did that ages ago. :l Thank you for bringing this up. I'll fix it posthaste.

Edit: On second check, there's a name there already. Nevermind.

item.title: Re: Listing
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the author doesn't appear to have slotted a name in yet. I'm guessing they will, eventually.

item.title: Listing
item.content:encoded:
Hi,

I noticed on the site listing that this article is still titled "[ACCESS DENIED]" like the other SCPs that don't have an article tied to them yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very funny, the arguments are what got me to upvote- that toilet has quite the vocabulary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Silly enough that I enjoyed it, but I don't know. It's just not clicking with me.

Neutral vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's phrased like if you enter it then 48 hours later, regardless of location, the effect happens. But then it says that the objects try to dissuade you from leaving, in a way that I wasn't sure if it was misphrased and meant "try to keep you in" or "try to keep you out" but they do SOMETHING involving wanting you in the house for 48 hours. But then if you have to stay in for 48 hours, how did the D-class get changed, no way they're leaving him in there for 48 hours just to change the recorders. But then if it's just 48 hours from entry regardless of where you are, how do they get the things out of the house?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just curious, do things have to stay in for the whole 48 hours to be affected? What if they're taken out and in again? On that note, do the hours have to be consecutive?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2257-2: It's—

Monty Python's Flying Circus!

Seriously, oldest lede-burying trick in the book. Took me right out of and otherwise wonderful story. So many other choices…

0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice concept. Kept me interested and left me wanting more. I would love to see more interviews, as the last interview was sort of goofy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the D class naked? If not, the god of orange jumpsuits needs some lines, or at least a mention, like "SCP-whatever remained silent".
Because all I could imagine is a naked D class with talking shoes… Sorta distracting =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That house looks nothing like something you'd find in Wisconsin. The lawn and architecture are more in line with something in the southwestern United States.

Like it a lot otherwise, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun. I feel like a Beauty And The Beast musical number is gonna break out any second.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted then. Thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Laughing so hard I can't find the upvote button.

There it is. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goofy as hell, but that final addendum really helps bring everything together.

When objects and entities enter SCP-2257 that are not currently an instance of SCP-2257-1 enter the premises,

Might wanna clean that up, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. The last interview was a little too goofy for me as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still love this. It's funny, but not at the expense of being a serious article, if that makes sense. The ID badge part you added is great and I'm proud to have inspired the shoe argument. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does it lack?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting premise, but it doesn't feel fully realized. The description is written decently, and it is amusing in an absurd sort of way, but it lacks in delivering a fully realized article. 0 voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I must have missed that bit, much appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was an XXXX left, but I fixed it. Per te questo e altro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, the holy war of words would literally ensue.

No, the identity is NOT that. That's an old thing used in SCP Series I that I've been told is no longer required, nor good practice. X3 It still makes me smirk, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have downvoted if not for the arguments. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kudos to TwistedGears, Voct, and crew on #site19 for the support and massive hilarity while writing this scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe I've fixed the discrepancies stated, and, to answer your questions:

Yes, there is a proper fine dining setup for a table.

They don't understand the concept of inanimate objects not being able to react. This is all they've ever known.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just too goddamn goofy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted already, but I am curious - what happens if an item is left long enough to become an instance of 2257-1, but another does already exist? If, say, you brought a chair into the house and left it? Would the chairs begin a holy war of words (since they can't move)?

Also, is the created identity (registered as the owner of the house) one Simon C. Patterson?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here we go.

+ show block
– hide block

the house repainted biannually to give the appearance of occupancy.

Who repaints their house every 6 months? A good coat of paint lasts for decades.

SCP-2257 is a white, one bedroom, two bathroom home in a suburban development located in ██████, Wisconsin. The exterior presents no anomalous properties. The interior is normally furnished for a suburban home, albeit that only one occurrence of any object, such as an armchair, exists within it.

The house in the picture isn't white, it's tan, and peach colored.

The statement "albeit that only one occurrence of any object, such as an armchair, exists within it." Feels like a tone break to explain that only one item of furniture of each type is present on the premises.

instances of SCP-2257-1 become agitated until they are able to return to the formal cutlery arrangement by dining standards in the United States of America (the salad fork must be on the far left, the dinner fork beside it, etc).

Dining standards? Is there such a thing?

All instances of SCP-2257-1 are capable of rearranging themselves when left unobserved. When objects and entities enter SCP-2257 that are not currently an instance of SCP-2257-1 enter the premises, instances will try to deter the subject from remaining within the home until the 48-hour threshold has passed, at which point the new instance is welcomed, so long as another occurrence of the same object does not already exist. Instances of SCP-2257-1 will rearrange themselves in ineffectual attempts to menace or otherwise deter objects that are deemed useless or extraneous.

But you said there aren't any living instances, how do we know they would be welcomed? Also how does one menace inanimate objects? If we drop a coffee maker in there, how could it be intimidated into leaving? It's a coffee maker!

Closing Statement: Interviewer rose from SCP-2257-1-23 without incident. SCP-2257-1-23 continues on a rant to attempt to draw interviewer back to it, but fails to convince Doctor B█████. The chair is reported to be comfortable, but not extraordinarily so, defined when off-site as an average armchair.

I thought entrants were deterred from staying. Also I got a good laugh out of that interview log. "I Bless your leavings!"

The conversion a living entity into an instance under SCP-2257 was previously presumed impossible. However, on 2/25/2014, SCP-2257-2 was created. SCP-2257-2 is a former D-Class, now referred to by other instances of SCP-2257-1 as "The Guardian", and is the result of a need to replace recording equipment continually converting into instances of SCP-2257-1. Conversion occurred unexpectedly after normal 48-hour threshold.

I would consider the fact that a living instance was created to be important enough to mention in the description, yet you put it waaaaaaaaay down here under 2 interview logs. It might alleviate things if you put the logs under collapsibles so we could see it or at the very least include a see addendum (insert addendum here) int he description.

Doctor B█████: Who are you? SCP-2257-2: The Gatekeeper.

I'm sorry…just…I'm sorry I can't help but think of ghostbusters.

In summation, it's mostly good, but has some annoying inconsistencies. I do like how the objects inside act all petty and the bit about the level 3 badge trying to be too big for his britches was funny.

Fix these and You got my upvote.
